annotations.label: Taylor's theorem (informal description)

anchor.snippet: Taylor's theorem gives an approximation of a

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Taylor's theorem (approximation by the degree-n Taylor polynomial) via taylorWithinEval and the remainder theorems such as taylor_mean_remainder_lagrange.

annotations.label: Linear approximation

anchor.snippet: then it has a linear approximation near this point

annotations.note: hasDerivAt_iff_isLittleO states exactly that differentiability is equivalent to a linear approximation with little-o error, i.e. the first-order Taylor approximation.

annotations.label: Quadratic approximation

anchor.snippet: we can fit a quadratic polynomial instead of a linear function

annotations.note: The quadratic approximation is the n=2 case of the general Taylor polynomial taylorWithinEval.

annotations.label: Quadratic more accurate than linear

anchor.snippet: more accurate than the linear approximation

annotations.note: The error orders o((x-x₀)ⁿ) are formalized in taylor_isLittleO, which underlies this comparison, but the qualitative 'more accurate' statement itself is not a theorem.

annotations.label: Error of degree-k approximation

anchor.snippet: the error in approximating a function by a polynomial of degree k will go to zero

annotations.note: taylor_isLittleO proves f x − taylorWithinEval f n s x₀ x = o((x−x₀)ⁿ), i.e. the degree-n approximation error vanishes faster than (x−x₀)ⁿ.

annotations.label: Asymptotic nature of Taylor's theorem

anchor.snippet: Taylor's theorem is of asymptotic nature

annotations.note: taylor_isLittleO (and taylor_tendsto) capture precisely the asymptotic statement that the remainder is o((x−x₀)ⁿ) as x→x₀.

annotations.label: Taylor polynomial

anchor.snippet: The polynomial appearing in Taylor's theorem is the

annotations.note: taylorWithin (and its evaluation taylorWithinEval) define the Taylor polynomial ∑ₖ f⁽ᵏ⁾(x₀)/k! (x−x₀)ᵏ.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of asymptotic best-fit polynomial

anchor.snippet: is the unique "asymptotic best fit" polynomial

annotations.note: Mathlib has no theorem characterizing the Taylor polynomial as the unique polynomial whose remainder is o((x−x₀)ⁿ).

annotations.label: Remainder term

anchor.snippet: Taylor's theorem describes the asymptotic behavior of the remainder term

annotations.note: The remainder appears throughout as the expression f x − taylorWithinEval f n s x₀ x, but there is no standalone named definition of Rₖ.

annotations.label: Taylor's theorem (Peano form)
annotations.note: taylor_isLittleO gives the Peano-form conclusion but under the stronger hypothesis ContDiffOn (C^n on a convex set) rather than mere k-fold differentiability at the point.

annotations.label: General mean value form of the remainder

anchor.snippet: This version covers the Lagrange and Cauchy forms of the remainder as special cases

annotations.note: taylor_mean_remainder is the general mean-value form (with auxiliary g) from which the Lagrange and Cauchy forms are derived as special cases.

annotations.label: Mean-value forms of the remainder

anchor.value: Mean-value forms of the remainder — Let

annotations.note: Both the Lagrange form (taylor_mean_remainder_lagrange) and Cauchy form (taylor_mean_remainder_cauchy) are formalized.

annotations.label: Integral form of the remainder
annotations.note: taylor_integral_remainder gives f x − Pₙ = ∫ₓ₀ˣ (x−t)ⁿ/n! • f⁽ⁿ⁺¹⁾(t) dt for C^{n+1} functions.

annotations.label: Remainder estimate from derivative bounds

anchor.snippet: Then the remainder term satisfies the inequality

annotations.note: taylor_mean_remainder_bound bounds the remainder by C·(x−a)^{n+1}/n! given a bound C on the (n+1)th derivative (vector-valued; the constant uses n! rather than (n+1)!).

annotations.label: Uniform estimate

anchor.snippet: The second inequality is called a uniform estimate

annotations.note: exists_taylor_mean_remainder_bound provides a single constant C bounding the remainder by C·(x−a)^{n+1} uniformly over the interval.

annotations.label: Approximating the exponential function

anchor.snippet: Suppose that we wish to find the approximate value of the function

annotations.note: Mathlib has Real.exp_bound estimating the error of the exponential's Taylor partial sums, but not this specific worked numerical example.

annotations.label: Real analytic function

anchor.section: Taylor expansions of real analytic functions
anchor.snippet: is real analytic if it is locally defined by a convergent power series

annotations.note: AnalyticAt (and AnalyticOnNhd) define a function as locally given by a convergent power series.

annotations.label: Cauchy–Hadamard formula

anchor.section: Taylor expansions of real analytic functions
anchor.snippet: the radius of convergence of a power series can be computed from the Cauchy–Hadamard formula

annotations.note: radius_inv_eq_limsup (and radius_eq_liminf) is the Cauchy–Hadamard theorem expressing the radius via limsup ‖pₙ‖^{1/n}.

annotations.label: Uniform convergence of power series

anchor.section: Taylor expansions of real analytic functions
anchor.snippet: it must converge uniformly on the closed interval

annotations.note: HasFPowerSeriesOnBall.tendstoUniformlyOn proves the partial sums converge uniformly on any closed ball strictly inside the radius of convergence.

annotations.label: Taylor polynomials of analytic functions

anchor.section: Taylor expansions of real analytic functions
anchor.snippet: The Taylor polynomials of the real analytic function f at a are simply the finite truncations

annotations.note: The Taylor polynomials are taylorWithin (equivalently FormalMultilinearSeries.partialSum of the power series), the finite truncations of the series.

annotations.label: Convergence of Taylor series

anchor.section: Taylor's theorem and convergence of Taylor series
anchor.snippet: The Taylor series of f will converge in some interval in which all its derivatives are bounded

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the Taylor series of an analytic function converges to it on its ball, but not the specific 'uniformly bounded derivatives ⟹ convergence' criterion.

annotations.label: Taylor series (infinite order Taylor polynomial)

anchor.section: Taylor's theorem and convergence of Taylor series
anchor.snippet: as its "infinite order Taylor polynomial" at a

annotations.note: The Taylor series is built via FormalMultilinearSeries.ofScalars with coefficients iteratedDeriv n f x / n!, but there is no dedicated named real 'Taylor series' definition.

annotations.label: Uniform remainder bound

anchor.section: Taylor's theorem and convergence of Taylor series
anchor.snippet: then for any order k and for any r > 0 there exists a constant

annotations.note: exists_taylor_mean_remainder_bound gives exactly: for each order n there exists a constant C with ‖Rₙ‖ ≤ C·(x−a)^{n+1} on the interval.

annotations.label: Uniform convergence to analytic limit

anchor.section: Taylor's theorem and convergence of Taylor series
anchor.snippet: the Taylor series of f converges uniformly to some analytic function

annotations.note: Uniform convergence of the series (tendstoUniformlyOn) and analyticity of the sum exist separately, but not packaged as this combined statement.

annotations.label: Non-analytic smooth (flat) function

anchor.section: Taylor's theorem and convergence of Taylor series
anchor.snippet: we say f is a non-analytic smooth function , for example a flat function

annotations.note: expNegInvGlue is smooth (expNegInvGlue.contDiff) yet not analytic at 0 (expNegInvGlue.not_analyticAt_zero), the standard flat non-analytic example.

annotations.label: Complex generalization of Taylor's theorem

anchor.snippet: Taylor's theorem generalizes to functions

annotations.note: A holomorphic function admits a local power-series (Taylor) expansion: DifferentiableOn.analyticOnNhd (and Differentiable.hasFPowerSeriesOnBall).

annotations.label: Cauchy's integral formula representation

anchor.snippet: Then Cauchy's integral formula with a positive parametrization

annotations.note: This is the Cauchy integral formula ∮ f(z)/(z−w) dz = 2πi·f(w) for differentiable f.

annotations.label: Complex differentiability implies infinite differentiability

anchor.snippet: then it is actually infinitely many times complex differentiable on U

annotations.note: DifferentiableOn.contDiffOn (and Differentiable.contDiff) proves a complex differentiable function on an open set is C^∞ there.

annotations.label: Cauchy's estimate

anchor.snippet: One also obtains Cauchy's estimate

annotations.note: This bounds ‖f⁽ⁿ⁾(c)‖ ≤ n!·C/Rⁿ from boundary values, i.e. Cauchy's estimate for derivatives.

annotations.label: Complex differentiable implies complex analytic

anchor.snippet: is in fact complex analytic

annotations.note: DifferentiableOn.analyticOnNhd / Differentiable.analyticAt prove complex differentiability implies analyticity.

annotations.label: Boundary domination of approximation quality

anchor.snippet: is dominated by the values of the function f itself on the boundary

annotations.note: The phenomenon is captured by Cauchy's estimate bounding derivatives (hence the remainder) via boundary values, but the explicit remainder-domination statement is not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Meromorphic extension and radius of convergence

anchor.snippet: is real analytic , that is, locally determined by its Taylor series

annotations.note: This specific example (1/(1+x²) and its radius of convergence determined by complex poles) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Differentiability in several variables

anchor.snippet: is differentiable at a ∈ R n if and only if there exists a linear functional L

annotations.note: HasFDerivAt defines differentiability at a via a continuous linear map with little-o error, generalizing to arbitrary normed spaces.

annotations.label: Differential of f at a

anchor.snippet: is the (uniquely defined) differential of f at the point a

annotations.note: fderiv is the (unique) Fréchet derivative / differential of f at a point.

annotations.label: Multi-index notation

anchor.snippet: Introduce the multi-index notation

annotations.note: Mathlib does not use multi-index notation for derivatives; it works with iteratedFDeriv (symmetric continuous multilinear maps) instead.

annotations.label: k times differentiable at a point

anchor.snippet: Then we say that f is k times differentiable at the point a

annotations.note: Mathlib's ContDiffAt captures k-times continuous differentiability via iteratedFDeriv, slightly stronger than mere k-fold differentiability at a point.

annotations.label: Multivariate exact remainder formula

anchor.snippet: then one can derive an exact formula for the remainder

annotations.note: Mathlib's Taylor theorem is one-dimensional (ℝ → E); the file explicitly lists higher-dimensional generalization as a TODO.

annotations.label: Multivariate uniform estimates

anchor.snippet: one immediately obtains the uniform estimates

annotations.note: No multivariate Taylor remainder estimates are formalized; only the one-dimensional case exists.

annotations.label: Multivariate version of Taylor's theorem
annotations.note: There is no multivariate Taylor's theorem in Mathlib; the Taylor file targets only ℝ → E and notes higher dimensions as a TODO.

annotations.label: Third-order Taylor polynomial in two dimensions

anchor.snippet: the third-order Taylor polynomial of a smooth function

annotations.note: This two-dimensional worked example is not formalized, as multivariate Taylor polynomials are absent from Mathlib.
